name: Magnesium
name_origin: Named after Magnesia, a district of Thessaly in Greece where magnesium-bearing minerals were originally found.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Magnesium is the eighth most abundant element in Earth's crust (~2.3%) and the third most abundant element dissolved in seawater. It does not occur as a free metal in nature; it is found in minerals like dolomite, magnesite, and carnallite.

compounds.name: Magnesium oxide
compounds.summary: Magnesia; a refractory material with a very high melting point used in furnace linings and as an antacid.

compounds.name: Magnesium chloride
compounds.summary: Used as a de-icer, dust suppressant, and feedstock for producing magnesium metal by electrolysis.

compounds.name: Magnesium hydroxide
compounds.summary: Milk of magnesia; a common antacid and laxative as well as a flame retardant.

compounds.name: Magnesium sulfate
compounds.summary: Epsom salt; used in bath salts, agriculture, and to treat magnesium deficiency or eclampsia.

compounds.name: Magnesium carbonate
compounds.summary: Used by athletes as gripping chalk and as a food additive (anti-caking agent).

compounds.name: Chlorophyll a
compounds.summary: The green pigment in plants that captures light for photosynthesis; magnesium is the central coordinating atom.

uses: Lightweight structural alloys (with aluminum) for aerospace, automotive, and electronic housings
Pyrotechnics, flares, and incendiary devices because it burns with an intense white flame
Reducing agent in the production of titanium, uranium, and other metals (Kroll process)
Sacrificial anodes for cathodic protection of steel structures and pipelines
Dietary mineral supplements and antacid/laxative pharmaceuticals
Desulfurizer in iron and steel production
reactivity_summary: Magnesium is a relatively reactive alkaline earth metal that tarnishes slightly in air, forming a thin protective oxide layer. It burns in air with a brilliant white light, reacting with both oxygen and nitrogen, and reacts slowly with cold water but vigorously with steam.
isotopes_summary: Magnesium has three stable isotopes: Mg-24 (~79%), Mg-25 (~10%), and Mg-26 (~11%). Mg-28 is the most stable radioisotope with a half-life of about 21 hours.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Magnesium
